mnemonics: Recoverable: Commit Before Commit (CBC) - A transaction commits only after all transactions it read from have committed.
Cascadeless: Read Committed Data (RCD) - A transaction can only read data written by transactions that have already committed.
Strict: Write Committed Data (WCD) - A transaction can only write to data items that were last written by committed transactions.
keyPoints: A recoverable schedule ensures no committed transaction reads data from an aborted transaction, preventing dirty reads.
A cascadeless schedule (or avoiding cascading rollbacks) ensures a transaction only reads committed data, so if Tj reads from Ti and Ti aborts, Tj does not need to be rolled back.

A strict schedule ensures a transaction can only read or overwrite the most recent data written by committed transactions, simplifying recovery by allowing undo operations only on uncommitted transactions.
commonMistakes: Assuming that a serializable schedule is automatically recoverable; while serializability is about correctness, recoverability is a separate property for fault tolerance.
Confusing cascadeless with strict; all strict schedules are cascadeless, but not all cascadeless schedules are strict (strict is stronger).
Thinking that a schedule must be strict to be recoverable; a schedule can be recoverable without being cascadeless or strict, but it's a weaker guarantee.
